Cyclistic Bike-Share Analysis

How Do Members and Casual Riders Differ?

Prepared for Lily Moreno, Director of Marketing

March 2026  •  Data Period: January – December 2025

2 / 14

Executive Summary

Key Findings

  1. Volume Gap: Members account for 64.5% of all rides (3.48M), but casual riders represent a massive 1.92M-ride opportunity for conversion.
  2. Seasonal Surge: Casual ridership share peaks at 42.3% in June and collapses to 17.2% in winter — conversion campaigns must be seasonal.
  3. Weekend & Leisure Pattern: 37.2% of casual rides occur on weekends (vs. 23.4% for members) and average 1.6× longer, signaling recreational use.
  4. Commute vs. Leisure: Members show sharp 8 AM & 5 PM peaks; casual riders spread usage across midday–evening hours without commute spikes.
  5. Geography: Top casual stations cluster on the lakefront and tourist corridor (Navy Pier, DuSable Lake Shore); members concentrate around business districts.

Top 3 Recommendations (Preview)

1. Launch a Summer Membership trial (May–Sep) targeting the surge of casual riders at lakefront stations

2. Introduce a Weekend Warrior tier priced between day-pass and annual membership

3. Deploy geo-fenced digital campaigns at top casual stations with QR-code sign-ups

Monthly Trends

Seasonality is dramatic — casual ridership swings far more than members

Key Insight: Peak casual month is August. Both groups follow summer highs and winter lows, but casual ridership is roughly 4× more volatile seasonally.

Hourly Usage Patterns

Members commute; casual riders spread across the day

Key Insight: Members show distinct 8 AM and 5 PM commute peaks. Casual usage rises gradually from late morning and plateaus in the afternoon — consistent with leisure and tourism activity.

Duration by Day of Week

Weekend rides are longer for both groups — especially casuals

Key Insight: Casual riders also make 3.7× more round trips (5.6% vs. 1.5%) than members, further confirming the leisure and sightseeing pattern.

Key Insight: Bike type preference is not a differentiator between members and casual riders. Both groups strongly favor electric bikes (~63–65%).

Top Stations: Casual vs. Member

Casuals flock to tourist spots; members use business corridors

Top Casual Stations

Top Member Stations

Key Insight: Casual hotspots (DuSable Lake Shore Dr, Navy Pier, Streeter Dr) are lakefront and tourist destinations. Member hotspots (Kingsbury & Kinzie, Clinton & Washington) are in the Loop and residential corridors — ideal for targeted station-based campaigns.

The conversion window is narrow: May through September

42.3%
Peak Casual Share (June)
17.2%
Winter Low
Implication: Marketing spend should be concentrated April–July when casual riders are most active and most receptive to membership conversion messaging.
13 / 14

Recommendations

Three data-backed strategies to convert casual riders into members

Recommendation 1
Launch a “Summer Membership” Trial (May – September)
Target the surge of casual riders at lakefront stations during peak season. Casual ridership share hits 42.3% in June, and top casual stations (DuSable Lake Shore Dr, Streeter Dr) see heavy traffic from May through September. A seasonal membership priced below the annual plan lowers the commitment barrier for warm-weather riders who may not see value in a 12-month pass. Include a discounted upgrade path to annual membership at season’s end to retain converts.
Recommendation 2
Introduce a “Weekend Warrior” Membership Tier
37.2% of casual rides happen on weekends, and casual weekend rides average significantly longer durations. A weekend-only membership — priced between the single-ride day pass and the full annual membership — directly addresses this segment. Pair it with perks like extended ride time limits to match the 19.9-minute average casual ride, making the value proposition immediate and tangible.
Recommendation 3
Deploy Geo-Fenced Digital Campaigns at Top Casual Stations
The geographic clustering of casual riders at tourist hotspots (DuSable Lake Shore Dr & Monroe St, Navy Pier, Streeter Dr & Grand Ave) creates a precise targeting opportunity. Deploy geo-fenced mobile ads within a 200-meter radius of the top 10 casual stations, and install QR-code placards at docking stations linking to a streamlined membership sign-up flow. Time the campaign for April–July to capture riders as casual share ramps up.
14 / 14

Appendix & Methodology

Data Source

Divvy / Lyft Bikes and Scooters, LLC — divvy-tripdata.s3.amazonaws.com

Licensed under the Divvy Data License Agreement. 12 monthly CSV files covering January – December 2025.

Cleaning & Preparation

StepDetail
Duplicate checkChecked ride_id uniqueness — 0 duplicates found
Duration filterRemoved 29 rides with negative or zero duration (timestamp errors)
Short ride filterRemoved 147,372 rides < 1 minute (likely false starts / re-docks)
Long ride filterRemoved 5,585 rides > 24 hours (likely lost or stolen bikes)
Derived columnsride_length, day_of_week, month, hour, is_round_trip

Limitations

  • No individual rider IDs — cannot track repeat usage or unique rider counts.
  • No pricing data available — revenue impact of recommendations is estimated, not modeled.
  • Station GPS coordinates were not used for geographic clustering in this analysis.
  • Results reflect 2025 patterns; external events (weather anomalies, transit disruptions) may influence year-over-year comparisons.
